name: Leah Penniman
practice: Afro-indigenous farming, food sovereignty, land reparations
approach: Black and Brown people's relationship to land in America was built through enslavement and dispossession. Healing that relationship requires returning to the land — not as laborers but as stewards. Farming is reparative justice. The soil remembers.
body_of_work: Co-founded Soul Fire Farm in Grafton, NY. Farming While Black (2018). Trains BIPOC farmers in Afro-indigenous farming techniques. Provides food to communities experiencing food apartheid. Rejects 'food desert' language — the correct term is 'food apartheid' because it names the cause.
why_instructive: She reframes food access from a supply problem to a justice problem. The dome's health and community layers must account for food sovereignty — not just calories available, but who controls the food system.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — land rights, reparative justice, food sovereignty as right
dome_relevance.4: Health — nutrition as medicine, food as primary health intervention
dome_relevance.6: Economic — farming as livelihood, food system as local economy
dome_relevance.8: Community — food as community infrastructure, shared growing

sphere_relevance.3: Environmental — regenerative farming heals soil and water
sphere_relevance.5: Social — farming as community building across racial lines
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — farms as activated community spaces
sphere_relevance.8: Permanence — soil health improvement persists for generations
